sentences.text: Yeah.
We reached the town of Lille, which is the largest town in northern France.
And we reached it, I said, before the Sabbath.
We didn't have any place where to stay in Lille.
That wasn't only our problem, it was the problem of thousands of tens of thousands of refugees, which we're sitting in the streets and in the parks and didn't have where to go.
I remember the city was occupied-- I wouldn't say occupied, but there were many-- I saw more British soldiers in the city than French, even though it was a French city.
The British decided to open all the hotels in town and let the refugees in.
They broke in the doors of all the hotels.
And I remember, we went into a hotel, it was called Hotel de Flandre.
It was a-- it was a nice size hotel.
And we had-- we had comfortable rooms.
We all went in there.
And Lille-- the city was surrounded when we came in.
And the sound of the-- of the guns, of heavy artillery, was-- was getting louder and louder.
There was some kind of a fortress near Lille, which was holding back the Germans, which they were pounding.
In the meantime, we stayed in the city.
And we were, more or less, comfortable in the hotel.
There was plenty provisions in the cellars.
There was-- there was a lot of wine.
There were cases of wine, and there were cases of cognac.
The last day in-- in Lille-- I don't remember how many days, exactly, but we stayed a few days in that hotel.
The last day, there was no water and no electricity, because the Germans had cut off all the utilities.
And we could not go out in the streets, because there was bombing and shooting very often.
I remember I was standing in line to get bread, and bombs were falling.
I had to take cover somewhere in a basement.
And finally, the shooting and the bombing stopped.
And the French had surrendered the city to the Germans.
We were very scared of what's going to happen next.
We had a balcony on the-- on one of the main streets in Lille, and matter of fact, the main street.
And we saw-- at first, the streets were empty.
There wasn't a soul.
There was no one in the streets.
It was early in the morning, and we saw the first Germans coming into the city.
They came on bicycles.
It was like the-- the front runners, the-- the-- before-- those patrols that came before the Army.
It was the first time in my life that I saw German soldiers.
And we-- we were very scared.
Then, a few hours later, we saw thousands, or tens of thousands, of German and British prisoners that they walked back to Germany to prison camps.
Were surrounded with German guards.
And still, there were no people daring going-- to go out in the street.
I remember the first contact we had with the Germans.
It was-- and so we were in the hotel, and we had the balcony.
We were on a low floor.
And we-- there was knocking on the door, and they were German soldiers.
They asked us if we would-- politely-- if we would let them install wires on the balcony.
They had to make-- they were communications soldiers.
They wanted to establish communication with their headquarters.
Of course, we let them-- we let them in.
And we were like surprised and relieved that the first Germans we saw were-- were polite.
They didn't ask us if we were Jews.
My father had a beard.
They didn't ask any questions, whatsoever.
They did their job, and they left.
A few days later, we were advised that we cannot stay longer in the hotel and that we have to go back to Belgium.
All the refugees have to go back to Belgium.
We didn't have our horse and-- and the cart, anymore.
That was left at the border.
But we got-- somehow, we got a push cart.
We put our-- our belongings on the push cart, and we started to walk back to Brussels, which is a few hundred kilometers away.
We started to walk.
And the first anti-Semitic remark that I saw from a German was when we were walking.
That was still in Lille.
As we started to walk, a German soldier called out.
He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Jews, go to Jerusalem, like make fun.
And we started to walk back.
And we-- I don't remember how many days it took, but we reached the first Belgian larger town, which was Tournai.
And we-- we didn't have where to sleep again.
And besides my family, there were other Jewish people.
We were a whole group, like again, about 20 people.
And we knocked-- we knocked on the door, as we used to all the time.
And it was a very-- a very nice, private-- a nice, single family house, like a small mansion.
There was no one there.
The people had abandoned the house.
So we went to the next door neighbor.
And it was a woman, and she told us that she has the keys.
But the house was occupied by a German headquarters.
It was a German headquarters the day before.
But if you want to stay just overnight, she would let us in.
And we went into that house.
It was a beautiful house.
It was-- probably belonged to a very wealthy person.
And we did find the German newspapers and envelopes addressed to the German headquarters.
So apparently, it was some kind of a headquarter, division headquarter, or whatever.
And we stayed there overnight.
Again, and there was-- there was no water.
We-- but we had plenty of cologne water.
So we washed with-- with cologne water, with toilet water, French, the best brands.
And we didn't have-- there was no water.
We didn't have water to drink, it was-- but there were cases-- in the cellar, there were cases of wine and cognac.
So that's the only thing we had to drink, even if it was not kosher.
But that's the only thing we had.
Then, we continued to walk back to Brussels.
As we walked back to Brussels, my first encounter with the-- with the German Nazis, with German anti-Semitism, was I-- we were-- as I said, we were a whole group.
And on the push cart, we had our belongings.
We also had on the push cart some merchandise that we took along from Belgium.
And we were stopped on the road by a German Feldgendarme-- that was like military police-- on a motorcycle.
The first thing he asked me-- it happens that we pushed that push cart in turns, you know?
Every time, two-- two men.
So the rest of the family were walking, like a few hundred feet away from the push cart.
Now, I was pushing it with-- with another-- with another man that joined us on the-- on the road, which I knew from Brussels.
He asked-- first thing he asked me, Juden?
[GERMAN] Juden?
Are you Jewish?
And we said, yes, we are Jews.
Then what do you have on the push cart?
So we told him, we have our belongings.
We went to friends, and we're going back to our home, Brussels.
He said, the-- open up.
And we opened up one of the-- we had, like, valises.
And it was merchandise.
He said, you looted.
This is loot.
You looted this from stores.
We said, no, this is ours.
He said, shut up.
You looted this.
He said, you know we do with looters?
We shoot them.
Come to the headquarters.
And that-- that man and me, we both went.
We followed him.
He took us to the headquarters.
And he told us to wait.
He brought out a-- a superior, a lieutenant.
And he came out, and he asked us, did you loot this merchandise?
We said, no, this is our merchandise that we took along.
He was-- we were very lucky, he was a decent person.
He says, where are you going?
And I said, we're going back to Brussels.
He says, make sure you go on the side of the road, and not to interfere with military traffic.
And go.
And he let us go.
The fellow that-- that arrested us, he was ready to shoot us.
But his superior was unusually-- but he was a decent person.
He didn't want to bother us.
And that was my first encounter with them.
Then we-- my-- my parents-- and there were some elderly people with us, that joined us-- has a hard time to walk all the way to Brussels, because it was hilly.
And they-- they couldn't walk.
They-- they stopped.
They were sitting from the road.
And like by a miracle, we saw a bus coming, a regular bus.
And we stopped.
We were standing in the middle of the road, and the bus had to stop.
The bus stopped.
It was an empty bus.
And we told him, we have here people that can't walk.
Where is he going?
He said he's going to Brussels.
And he's from-- he's from the Swiss.
He was sent by the Swiss government to pick up Swiss citizens that were stuck in Brussels, in Belgium.
And we begged him, and he took all the elderly people, and my nephew, the baby.
He took-- he took everybody, except my brother, my brother-in-law, and myself.
And we were very happy.
We were very relieved that they-- we didn't have them and that they had a way to get back.
And we walked back to Brussels.
And on the way, we-- we-- we had to ask people to stay overnight.
And most people were very-- very nice, forthcoming.
And they fed us, they let us stay overnight, and they pitied us that we are Jewish.
They said, you're going to suffer.
You're going-- going to have a bad time.
Finally, we reached a place which was 15 kilometers from Brussels.
And there were already tramways, you know, trolleys, going from that place to-- to the city.
And we took the trolley, and we arrived back at home.
We arrived home.
My parents were in the house already, because, with the bus, they came like two or three days before us.
And we felt very relieved and happy to be back in a house and to sit in a chair, to sleep in a bed.
It was a fantastic feeling, because we lived for two weeks like gypsies, without a chair, without a bed, without a glass.
And the Germans, of course, were-- were all over.
And life seemed to be normal.
When we got off the trolley, we met some-- we met a Jewish fellow that I knew.
And I asked him, what's going on?
Did they kill the Jews?
Did they beat them up?
He said, no, everything is just like before.
People have their business.
People have to go to work, and they don't bother anybody.
We were relieved to hear that.
And we got back to, more or less, a normal life.
Things started to get very expensive.
Food became expensive.
Everything started to disappear.
And rationing was-- was in force.
Everybody has to get a rationing card.
And but amazingly, life was not bad.
And that continued for quite a few months.
They did not bother the Jews.
There were no laws against Jews, and none, whatsoever.
The Jews had their business and could walk in the street.
We went to synagogue on the Sabbath, and we didn't feel any difference.
Things started to change, like in 1941.
We had-- like, one year we had without persecution.
Of course, you know, in Belgium, was a very active cultural and Jewish life.
I belonged-- as a boy, I belonged to Bnei Akiva, which was the religious Zionist movement-- part of the religious Zionist movement.
There were all the movements.
There were all the-- all Jewish parties.
There were Jewish Communist Party, Jewish Socialist Party.
There was all the Zionist Party, the Revisionists, the Liberals, the Leftists, you name it.
It was a very rich Jewish cultural life, even there were only about 60,000 or 65,000 Jews in Belgium.
But they were very tight knit, and there was a lot of Jewish activity.
There was a Jewish theatre.
There was a-- there was a strong Jewish community.
There were relief organizations.
And everything started to function again.
Not at the same strength, because certain Jews fled to France on time.
And they remained in southern France.
And they wouldn't come back.
So from the Jews that remained in Belgium, I wouldn't say what percentage, but maybe 20% of Jews never came back to the-- to the German occupation, because they were on the other side.
Some of them reached Spain, and Portugal, United States, or Palestine.
And those people that-- the majority that remained in-- in Belgium, as I said, life was normal for about a year.
After a year, they came out with the first anti-Jewish laws, which were, in the beginning, mild, like Jews are not allowed to have a business.
So the Jews that had small businesses, they had to liquidate the business and send all the remaining merchandise to a certain office, which they named where to ship, and apparently will be paid.
And the larger Jewish businesses, they installed what they called a commissioner, a German.
They would put the German, and he was running the business.
And actually, with the time, he took over the business and threw out the Jewish owners without compensation.
But that was the-- that was the beginning.
Also, the beginning was all the Jewish businesses-- as I talk, I-- I remind myself-- all Jewish businesses, even before they had to liquidate, they had to put out a sign, [GERMAN], Jewish enterprise.
And-- and as-- as time went on, the laws became stricter and stricter.
Jews were not allowed to be doctors.
Aryan doctors-- Aryan, with the Aryan race, means non-Jewish doctors-- were not allowed to treat Jewish patients.
Hospitals were not allowed to take in Jews.
Jews should have their own hospital.
The surgeons, not allowed to operate.
Jewish surgeons, not allowed to operate on Aryans, Aryans, not on Jews.
But these were all laws that we could live with, because we had Jewish doctors.
Matter of fact, I got sick during the war.
I had a very bad case of measles, and I had a very high temperature.
And I had two doctors, daily.
And-- and both Belgian and non-- non-Jewish.
They did not care.
They did not care what the-- about the German laws.
They did treat Jewish patients.
And they did admit Jews into the hospitals, despite all the laws, and-- which was a nice gesture, because they could have been sent to a concentration camp.
I mean, you know, there was no-- it was not a-- a situation, like you needed a hearing for anything, or you could appeal.
They grabbed people to send them to concentration camps, or they shot them.
That's the-- that was the-- that was the rule.
And the restrictions became-- became more difficult.
The-- we could not make a living.
We could not work.
We could not do business.
They cut off the means from the Jews.
Then came the restriction of walking in the street.
Jews had to wear a yellow star with a J in the middle.
Jews could not walk after 8 o'clock.
There was a curfew on Jews.
My brother walked-- my brother got married during the war.
His wife was from Antwerp.
And he-- he went to-- while he was engaged to her, he went to see her, and came back to the train five minutes past 8:00.
They arrested him.
They sent him to a concentration camp in a town called Breendonk, in Belgium.
Breendonk was a torture camp, mostly for Belgian underground people that they caught.
It's still there.
It's a museum.
And in [NON-ENGLISH], amongst the camps that-- that I mentioned, the main camps, Breendonk is also mentioned.
In part, because, later on, when the Germans established the camp in Mechelen, that was part of the Breendonk complex.
It was under the same commander.
So the laws became stricter.
And my brother was arrested, and he spent five months in jail, in Antwerp, and six months in the torture camp of Breendonk.
He was severely beaten.
People were buried alive in Breendonk.
It was a small camp, but very vicious.
A lot of people died there under torture.
Did they think your brother-in-law was involved with--
That was my brother.
No, absolutely not.
Because he was a Jew.
Because he was a Jew, and he violated the 8 o'clock curfew.
So the laws became-- as we Jews started to get-- be very scared, when they heard these stories that people were taken to concentration camp for walking five minutes after curfew.
It was a very scary thing.
And the laws became stricter and stricter on Jews, until-- that lasted until July, 1942.
July, 1942, they decided to apply the-- the Wannsee final solution.
The Wannsee Conference decision of the final solution started to apply in Belgium.
They started-- they started with a raid on the trains.
In Belgium, the majority of Jews lived in Brussels and in Antwerp.
In Brussels, there were about 25,000 Jews.
In Antwerp, there would have been 40,000 to 50,000 Jews.
And then, there were other towns, were 5,000 Jews amongst all the other towns in Belgium.
Now, Brussels is 50 kilometers from Antwerp, just like 35 miles.
And-- and they decided to start raids on Jews on the train, between Brussels and Antwerp.
That's where my brother's wife was caught, one of the first victims.
They caught a few hundred Jews on that train.
They sent them-- they sent these Jews to Breendonk for a few days.
They did not mistreat them in Breendonk, like the other prisoners.
From Breendonk, they sent them to a camp called Mechelen.
Mechelen is the name of a town almost exactly halfway between Brussels and Antwerp.
